---
title: "<small>36-315: Statistical Graphics and Visualization, Summer 2026</small>"
format: html
---

---

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Instructor**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
Quang Nguyen

Email: [`quang@stat.cmu.edu`](mailto:quang@stat.cmu.edu)

Office Hours: Wednesdays 2--3pm
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Website**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
[`36-315-summer26.netlify.app`](https://36-315-summer26.netlify.app/)
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Textbooks**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
None required. Lecture slides will be posted. Other references will be provided throughout the course.
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Software**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
**R** (programming language), **RStudio** (IDE), and  **Quarto** (reproducible documents)

:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Lectures**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
MWF 12:30--1:50pm, SH 234
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Labs**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
TR 12:30--1:50pm, BH 140B
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Weekly Assignments**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
Labs are due on Tuesdays/Thursdays by 11:59pm.<br>
Homeworks are due on Fridays by 6pm.
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Graphic Critiques**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
There will be two graphic critiques due Wednesday, May 27 and Wednesday, June 10.
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Take-Home Exam**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
There will be one take-home exam on Tuesday, June 2.
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Final Project**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}
Includes a video presentation and a project report, both due Thursday, June 18.
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-3}
**Grading**
:::

::: {.g-col-9}

25% &nbsp; &nbsp; Homework

15% &nbsp; &nbsp; Lab

20% &nbsp; &nbsp; Take-Home Exam

10% &nbsp; &nbsp; Graphics Critique

30% &nbsp; &nbsp; Final Project

Final grades are computed based on the weighting scheme above.

**No assignments are dropped when calculating grades.**

Final letter grades will be based on the standard 10-point scale
(A: 90--100, B: 80--89, C: 70--79, D: 60--69, R: 0--59).
Grades will be rounded, such that an 89.50% would be an A, but an 89.49% would not.

Final semester grades may be curved, but the curve will never lead to a worse grade than the 10-point scale.
For example, a grade of 85% would earn you at least a B, and a curve could only improve it.

:::

:::

---

<u><big>**Course Components**</big></u>

**1. Lectures**

Almost all course content will be covered during lecture.
Lectures will prepare you for subsequent assignments.
If you miss a lecture, you are responsible for the material covered.

Please participate during lecture.
And please make a habit of arriving on time out of respect for your classmates and the instructor.
Consistent tardiness is not acceptable and may lead to grade deductions.

**2. Labs**

Bi-weekly lab sessions give students opportunities to directly engage with the course materials.
Each lab will be turned in as a lab assignment.
Labs are designed to be completed during the session, but they are not due until 11:59 PM ET on the day of the lab, so students have extra time if needed.
**Labs must be completed in a Quarto document and submitted on Gradescope as a pdf file.**

Live lab attendance is **mandatory.**
As with lectures, please arrive to labs on time.
If you submit a lab assignment but did not show up to lab, there is an automatic **20 point** deduction.
However, if you need to miss a lab for a valid reason, contact me at least **36 hours** before the lab to discuss doing the lab on your own and getting full credit (i.e., if you need to miss a Tuesday lab, contact me by Sunday night; if you need to miss a Thursday lab, contact me by Tuesday night).
If there is an unexpected emergency, notify me immediately and I will determine if an accommodation is possible.

**3. Homeworks**

Homework assignments are due on Fridays by 6:00 PM ET, and will be posted on the Monday of the same week.
**Homeworks must be completed in a Quarto document and submitted on Gradescope as a pdf file.**
Be sure that your code, graphs, and answers are properly displayed on your homework before submitting.

**4. Graphics Critiques**

Data visualizations can be found everywhere.
In the course, you will turn in two critiques of data visualizations you find.
These must be from a recent source that was posted online for the first time within the **past year** (i.e., not from some 2011 blog post) and can **not** be an example from lecture, labs, or homework.

A graphics critique assignment template (using Quarto) will be provided.
You will get practice doing this on the first homework.
Then, you will submit two graphic critiques during the semester.
The due dates for each critique are **Wednesday, May 27, 11:59pm** and **Wednesday, June 10, 11:59pm**.
These are meant to be light and entertaining exercises.

**5. Take-Home Exam**

There is one take-home exam on **Tuesday, June 2**.
The exam will assess students’ ability to use material from the first-half of the semester to answer open-ended questions about a real dataset.
Students will have from **9AM to 11:59PM ET on Tuesday, June 2** to complete the exam.
The exam is designed to take 2--3 hours, but the 15-hour time window is only meant to provide some flexibility and alleviate time pressure.
More details will be given closer to the exam date.
Failure to submit on time is an automatic 0%.

**6. Final Project**

The purpose of this project is to assess students’ ability to create high-quality data visualizations and communicate their work in common formats: reports and presentations.
These communication skills are important in statistics and data science.
The deliverables can be part of your data science portfolio, as you
apply for jobs, graduate schools, and other opportunities.

Students will work individually to analyze a dataset of their choosing.
The project will culminate in a report and a short video recording.
More details about the project will be provided later.

---

<u><big>**Course Logistics and Policies**</big></u>

**Integrity.** Cheating, plagiarism, and/or unauthorized assistance on assignments will be dealt with in accordance with the [CMU Policy on Academic Integrity](https://www.cmu.edu/policies/student-and-student-life/academic-integrity.html). 

**Collaboration.**
Discussing assignments with your classmates is allowed and encouraged, and helping explain ideas to each other is a core part of the academic experience.
But it is important that every student get practice working on their own.
This means that **all the work you turn in must be your own**.
You must write your own code, generate your own graphics, and write your own solutions and reports.

**External Sources.**
External sources (e.g., books, websites, etc.) must be used to support your work, not to obtain your work.
You may use external sources to look up code documentation, find useful packages, find explanations for error messages, or clarify lecture materials.
You may not use them to copy code, text, or graphics without attribution.
Do not copy old solutions (in whole or in part), and do not consult or read them.

**Generative AI.** You may consult generative AI tools, but you must still take intellectual responsibility for your code, analysis, and writing, and be able to explain and defend every decision. You must do the thinking, not generative AI. You may not use generative AI to come up with analysis or interpretation for you, only to augment your own work. If you use a generative AI tool, you must disclose your use in your submission and explain what you used it for.

**Assignment Solutions.**
Homework and lab solutions will typically be posted the day after the assignment is due.
The provided detailed solutions will be helpful for future assignments.
Reviewing the solutions is important and helpful, even if you aced the assignment.
Assignments are typically graded within a week of when they are submitted.

**Regrade Requests.**
If you believe a mistake was made when your assignment was graded, *first* check the posted assignment solutions. If you still believe a mistake was made---after, and only after, reviewing the solutions---you may submit a regrade request on Gradescope. Do not make a request via email. In your regrade request, clearly (and politely) explain how the solutions indicate a possible grading mistake. This regrade request must be made **within one week** of when the assignment was graded. If a regrade request is made more than one week after grades are posted, it will not be processed.

**Extensions.**
In general, late assignments will not be accepted.
However, extensions may be granted for: illness, family emergencies, job interviews, or religious reasons.
To receive an extension, you must contact the instructor **36 hours** before the due date.
It is up to the instructor to decide if an extension may be granted.
Note that if your request does not involve an illness, family emergency, job interview, or religious reason, then it is extremely unlikely that an extension will be granted.

**Disability Services.** 
If you require a special accommodation (e.g., for exams, class notes, or other aspects of the class),
please visit the [Office of Disability Resources](https://www.cmu.edu/disability-resources/) to obtain appropriate
documentation. We cannot make any accommodations without documentation.

---

<u><big>**Course Timeline**</big></u> *(subject to change)*

| Date                    | Session            | Assignment              |
| ----------------------- | ------------------ | ----------------------- |
| **Week 1**              |                    |                         |
| May 11 (Monday)         | Lecture 1          |                         |
| May 12 (Tuesday)        | Lab 0              | Lab 0 due               |
| May 13 (Wednesday)      | Lecture 2          |                         |
| May 14 (Thursday)       | Lab 1              | Lab 1 due               |
| May 15 (Friday)         | Lecture 3          | Homework 1 due          |
| **Week 2**              |                    |                         |
| May 18 (Monday)         | Lecture 4          |                         |
| May 19 (Tuesday)        | Lab 2              | Lab 2 due               |
| May 20 (Wednesday)      | Lecture 5          |                         |
| May 21 (Thursday)       | Lab 3              | Lab 3 due               |
| May 22 (Friday)         | Lecture 6          | Homework 2 due          |
| **Week 3**              |                    |                         |
| May 25 (Monday)         | Memorial Day       |                         |
| May 26 (Tuesday)        | Lab 4              | Lab 4 due               |
| May 27 (Wednesday)      | Lecture 7          | Graphics Critique 1 due |
| May 28 (Thursday)       | Lab 5              | Lab 5 due               |
| May 29 (Friday)         | Lecture 8          | Homework 3 due          |
| **Week 4**              |                    |                         |
| June 1 (Monday)         | Lecture 9          |                         |
| June 2 (Tuesday)        | NO LAB             | Take-Home due           |
| June 3 (Wednesday)      | Lecture 10         |                         |
| June 4 (Thursday)       | Lab 6              | Lab 6 due               |
| June 5 (Friday)         | Lecture 11         | Homework 4 due          |
| **Week 5**              |                    |                         |
| June 8 (Monday)         | Lecture 12         |                         |
| June 9 (Tuesday)        | Lab 7              | Lab 7 due               |
| June 10 (Wednesday)     | Lecture 13         | Graphics Critique 2 due |
| June 11 (Thursday)      | Lab 8              | Lab 8 due               |
| June 12 (Friday)        | Lecture 14         | Homework 5 due          |
| **Week 6**              |                    |                         |
| June 15 (Monday)        | Lecture 15         |                         |
| June 16 (Tuesday)       | NO LAB             |                         |
| June 17 (Wednesday)     | Lecture 16         |                         |
| June 18 (Thursday)      | NO LAB             | Final Project due       |
| June 19 (Friday)        | Juneteenth         |                         |
